solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    bool canBeEqual(vector<int>& target, vector<int>& arr) {
        vector<int> sortedTarget = target;
        vector<int> sortedArr = arr;
        sort(sortedTarget.begin(), sortedTarget.end());
        sort(sortedArr.begin(), sortedArr.end());
        return sortedTarget == sortedArr;
    }
};
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n log n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The problem reduces to checking if both arrays contain the same elements. Sorting both arrays and comparing them directly verifies this. The approach is optimal for the given constraints. Key strength is simplicity and efficiency; weakness is O(n) space for copies, though acceptable for n ≤ 1000.
```
